6-0.q: The 3-way match in the Expenditure Cycle requires agreement between:
o: Invoice, cheque, and bank statement
Purchase Order, Receiving Report, and Vendor Invoice
Budget, actual, and variance report
Supplier quote, purchase order, and payment
6-0.e: 3-Way Match: Purchase Order (what was ordered and agreed price) + Receiving Report (what was actually received) + Vendor Invoice (what the vendor is charging). All three must agree before payment is approved. This prevents payment for goods not ordered or not received.

6-0.q: In the HR & Payroll Cycle, a key segregation of duties control requires that:
o: The same employee handles hiring and payroll
HR authorises new hires separately from the payroll department
Payroll is calculated by the same person who distributes cheques
Supervisors never review timesheets
6-0.e: Critical Sod in payroll: HR (authorises employment — who is on payroll) must be Separate from Payroll Processing (calculates and issues pay). If the same person controls both, they could add fictitious employees ('ghost employees') to the payroll and pocket the payments.

6-0.q: A Materials Requisition in the Production Cycle is used to:
o: Authorise payment to a supplier
Order goods from an external vendor
Authorise the release of raw materials from the storeroom to production
Record completed goods in finished inventory
6-0.e: Materials Requisition = internal document that Authorises the release of specific materials from the Storeroom to the Production floor for a specific job or production order. Without this authorisation, materials should not leave the storeroom — it creates an audit trail and controls inventory.

6-0.q: In the Revenue-to-Cash Cycle, which step occurs Before an invoice is sent?
o: Cash collection
Credit check and shipment of goods
Accounts receivable recording
Bank reconciliation
6-0.e: Revenue-to-Cash sequence: (1) Customer order received → (2) Credit Check → (3) Order approved → (4) Goods picked & Shipped (shipping document created) → (5) Invoice sent to customer → (6) Ar recorded → (7) Cash collected → (8) Cash applied to Ar.

6-0.q: All five transaction cycles ultimately feed into the:
o: Accounts Payable subsidiary ledger
Budget system
General Ledger — the central repository for all accounting entries
Sales order system
6-0.e: The General Ledger is the central hub — every transaction from all five cycles is ultimately posted to the Gl. Subsidiary ledgers (Ar, AP, inventory, payroll register) provide detail but reconcile to Gl control accounts. The Gl is the basis for financial statement preparation.

6-0.q: In the Financing Cycle, who must authorise new debt issuance before funds are accepted?
o: The accounting department
The external auditor
The Board of Directors
The payroll department
6-0.e: Board Of Directors must authorise significant financing activities — issuing debt, issuing equity, declaring dividends. This is a fundamental governance control ensuring major financial commitments are reviewed and approved at the highest level. Management cannot independently bind the company to major debt obligations.

6-0.q: Which document in the Revenue-to-Cash cycle authorizes the warehouse to pick goods for shipment?
o: Sales invoice
Bill of lading
Sales Order / Pick List — authorizes warehouse to pull and pack goods
Remittance advice
6-0.e: In the revenue cycle flow: Customer Po → Sales Order (internal authorization) → Pick List (tells warehouse what to pull) → Bill of Lading (shipping document) → Invoice (bill to customer) → Cash receipt. The pick list is the internal document that authorizes physical movement of goods from the warehouse.

6-0.q: The Bill Of Lading in the Revenue-to-Cash cycle serves as:
o: The customer's purchase order
An internal authorization to ship
A Legal Contract between the shipper and carrier that proves goods were shipped to the customer
The vendor invoice for freight costs
6-0.e: The Bill Of Lading is a legal shipping document that: (1) serves as a Contract between the shipper and carrier; (2) provides Evidence that goods were shipped; (3) specifies terms of shipment (quantity, destination, carrier). It is critical for revenue recognition — it is evidence that the performance obligation (delivery) was satisfied.

6-0.q: The Expenditure Cycle's primary purpose is to manage the process of:
o: Collecting cash from customers
Acquiring Goods And Services and paying vendors — the purchase-to-pay process
Recording employee payroll
Issuing new debt and equity
6-0.e: The Expenditure (Purchase-to-Pay) Cycle handles: requisition → purchase order → receipt of goods → vendor invoice → payment. Key controls: 3-way match (Po + Receiving Report + Invoice), segregation of purchasing from payment, approval hierarchies. The cycle creates accounts payable and ultimately cash disbursements.

6-0.q: In the Production Cycle, the Bill Of Materials (Bom) specifies:
o: The payment terms for raw material vendors
The schedule for manufacturing production runs
The Components And Quantities required to produce one unit of finished product
The cost of finished goods inventory
6-0.e: Bill Of Materials (Bom): a complete list of all Components, Raw Materials, and Quantities required to manufacture one unit of finished product. It drives: material requirements planning, raw material purchases (links production to expenditure cycle), standard cost calculations, and inventory management. Errors in the Bom propagate through all downstream systems.

6-0.q: The HR & Payroll Cycle begins with which transaction?
o: Issuing payroll checks
Computing employee tax withholdings
Hiring An Employee and entering their pay rate and deductions in the HR system
Recording accrued wages payable
6-0.e: The payroll cycle begins with HR establishing the employee master file (hiring, pay rate, deductions, benefits). Controls: HR sets up master data Independent of payroll processing. Sequence: HR authorization → time/attendance data → payroll calculation → payment → recording. The most critical control: only HR-authorized pay rates should be in the payroll system.

6-0.q: The Financing Cycle includes which of the following activities?
o: Collecting cash from customers for product sales
Paying employee salaries and benefits
Issuing Debt, Repaying Loans, Paying Dividends, and managing equity transactions
Purchasing inventory from suppliers
6-0.e: The Financing Cycle manages the organization's capital structure: (1) issuing bonds/notes payable and making repayments; (2) issuing equity (stock) and paying dividends; (3) managing treasury stock; (4) recording interest expense. Key controls: board authorization for significant financing transactions, segregation of cash custody from recording.

6-0.q: A Sub-Ledger in an Ais serves what purpose?
o: It replaces the general ledger for all transactions
It contains Detailed Records supporting a general ledger control account — the sub-ledger total must always equal the Gl control account balance
It is used only for inventory transactions
It records only non-cash transactions
6-0.e: Sub-Ledgers: detailed transaction records that support a Control Account in the general ledger. Examples: Accounts Receivable sub-ledger (individual customer balances) → Ar control account in Gl; AP sub-ledger (individual vendor balances) → AP control account in Gl; inventory sub-ledger → inventory Gl account. The sum of all sub-ledger balances Must equal the Gl control account — a key reconciliation control.

6-0.q: In the Revenue-to-Cash cycle, Remittance Advice is:
o: The document authorizing shipment of goods
The vendor's invoice sent to the customer
Information Accompanying A Customer Payment specifying which invoices are being paid — enables proper application of cash to the correct Ar balance
The bill of lading signed by the carrier
6-0.e: Remittance Advice accompanies a customer's payment and specifies: which invoices are being paid, the amounts, and any deductions (discounts, returns). Without remittance advice, cash application clerks may incorrectly apply payments. Controls: person receiving cash should send remittance advice to Ar department independently, while cash goes directly to treasury — implementing Sod.

6-0.q: The General Ledger & Reporting System acts as the integration point in an Ais because it:
o: Only records non-cash transactions
Processes all customer orders
Consolidates Journal Entries from All transaction cycles into a single trial balance for financial statement preparation
Manages only payroll transactions
6-0.e: The Gl & Reporting System is the hub of the Ais: it receives journal entries from All five transaction cycles (Revenue, Expenditure, Production, HR/Payroll, Financing) and consolidates them into the trial balance. From the trial balance, financial statements are prepared. Key controls: access restrictions to journal entry function, review of all journal entries, reconciliation of sub-ledgers to control accounts.

6-0.q: Which control Best prevents duplicate vendor payments in the Expenditure Cycle?
o: Requiring purchase orders for all purchases
Having the receiving department count goods
A System Check that identifies matching vendor number, invoice number, and amount before payment is approved
Reconciling the general ledger monthly
6-0.e: Duplicate payment prevention: the most effective control is a System-Based Duplicate Invoice Detection check that compares each new invoice to all previously paid invoices for the same vendor number, invoice number, and/or amount. This Prevents the duplicate payment Before it occurs. Monthly Gl reconciliation would only detect it afterward.

6-0.q: In an Ais, which document provides evidence that a customer Received the goods ordered?
o: Sales order
Customer purchase order
Signed Delivery Receipt or Proof Of Delivery — evidence the customer received the goods
Vendor invoice
6-0.e: Proof Of Delivery (signed delivery receipt or e-signature): evidence that the customer received the goods. This is critical for revenue recognition (performance obligation met), resolving customer claims ('I never received this'), and Ar collections. In many systems, electronic delivery confirmation (e.g., FedEx signature) is captured and attached to the sales transaction.

6-0.q: Turnaround Documents in an Ais are:
o: Documents destroyed after processing
Financial statements prepared at year-end
Machine-Readable Output sent to customers/suppliers that they return to trigger a transaction — combining output and input functions
Backup copies of accounting records
6-0.e: Turnaround Documents are output documents designed to be returned as input. Example: a utility bill with a tear-off payment stub containing a barcode — the customer returns the stub with payment. The barcode encodes account number and amount due, enabling automatic cash application when scanned. Reduces manual data entry and errors.

6-0.q: The Production Cycle links to the Expenditure Cycle primarily through:
o: Customer invoicing
Payroll processing
Purchases Of Raw Materials — production requirements drive material purchase orders to vendors
Dividend payments
6-0.e: The Production Cycle creates Material Requirements (from BOMs and production schedules) that drive purchase orders for raw materials in the Expenditure Cycle. Integration: Production plan → MRP calculates materials needed → Purchase requisitions sent to Purchasing → Purchase orders issued to vendors (Expenditure Cycle). This inter-cycle linkage is why integrated ERP systems are so valuable.

6-0.q: Which transaction cycle directly generates Wages Payable and Payroll Tax Liabilities on the balance sheet?
o: Revenue-to-Cash Cycle
Expenditure Cycle
Financing Cycle
HR & Payroll Cycle — accrued wages and employer payroll taxes flow to the balance sheet as current liabilities
6-0.e: The HR/Payroll Cycle generates: (1) Wages Payable — earned but unpaid wages (current liability); (2) Payroll Tax Liabilities — FICA, Futa, Suta withheld from employees or owed by employer; (3) Benefits Payable — health insurance, retirement contributions. These liabilities appear on the balance sheet until payment is made through the cash disbursement function.

6-1.q: The Four types of analytics in order of increasing complexity are:
o: Predictive, Descriptive, Diagnostic, Prescriptive
Descriptive, Diagnostic, Predictive, Prescriptive
Diagnostic, Descriptive, Prescriptive, Predictive
Prescriptive, Predictive, Diagnostic, Descriptive
6-1.e: The four types in order: (1) Descriptive — what happened? (2) Diagnostic — why did it happen? (3) Predictive — what will happen? (4) Prescriptive — what should we do? Complexity and value increase at each level. Most organizations are strong in descriptive but aspire to prescriptive analytics.

6-1.q: Big Data is characterized by the Three Vs, which are:
o: Value, Validity, Velocity
Volume, Velocity, Variety
Visibility, Verification, Volume
Value, Volume, Veracity
6-1.e: Big Data = 3 Vs: Volume (massive amounts of data), Velocity (generated and processed at high speed), Variety (structured, unstructured, and semi-structured formats). Traditional databases cannot handle Big Data — requires specialized tools like Hadoop, Spark.

6-1.q: An ERP system benefits an organization primarily by:
o: Replacing the need for internal controls
Integrating all business processes into a single shared database, eliminating data silos
Outsourcing all finance functions
Providing audit opinions on financial statements
6-1.e: ERP (Enterprise Resource Planning) integrates All business functions — finance, HR, supply chain, manufacturing, sales — into One shared database. This eliminates data silos, provides real-time data, improves accuracy, and automates processes.

6-1.q: Robotic Process Automation (Rpa) is best suited for:
o: Complex judgment-based financial analysis
Creative strategy development
Repetitive, Rule-Based tasks such as invoice processing and bank reconciliations
Customer relationship management
6-1.e: Rpa uses software robots to automate Repetitive, Rule-Based, high-volume tasks — invoice processing, data entry, bank reconciliations, report generation. Rpa is Not suitable for judgment-based or creative tasks.

6-1.q: The CIA Triad in cybersecurity stands for:
o: Control, Investigation, Accountability
Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability
Compliance, Integrity, Authorization
Custody, Independence, Assurance
6-1.e: CIA Triad: Confidentiality (data accessible only to authorized users), Integrity (data is accurate and not tampered with), Availability (systems accessible when needed). All three must be balanced.

6-1.q: The Correct order of the four types of analytics from Least to Most complex is:
o: Predictive → Descriptive → Diagnostic → Prescriptive
Descriptive → Diagnostic → Predictive → Prescriptive — each builds on the previous
Prescriptive → Predictive → Diagnostic → Descriptive
Diagnostic → Descriptive → Prescriptive → Predictive
6-1.e: Analytics progression: (1) Descriptive — 'What happened?' (reports, dashboards); (2) Diagnostic — 'Why did it happen?' (drill-down, root cause); (3) Predictive — 'What will happen?' (models, forecasting); (4) Prescriptive — 'What should we do?' (optimization, recommendations). Complexity and value increase across this spectrum — prescriptive is the most advanced but requires descriptive, diagnostic, and predictive as foundations.

6-1.q: Prescriptive analytics is Best illustrated by which example?
o: A dashboard showing last month's sales by region
Analyzing why Q3 revenue missed the forecast
Predicting which customers are likely to churn in the next 90 days
An Optimization Model that recommends the Optimal Product Mix to maximize contribution margin given capacity constraints
6-1.e: Prescriptive analytics goes beyond 'what will happen' to recommend the Best Course Of Action. Optimization models (linear programming, simulation) prescribe decisions. Examples: recommending the optimal product mix, determining the best inventory reorder point, suggesting the most profitable customer segments to target. It is the highest-value analytics tier.

6-1.q: Big Data is characterized by which Three dimensions (the '3 Vs')?
o: Validity, Velocity, Visualization
Volume (massive scale), Velocity (speed of generation), Variety (multiple data types and sources)
Value, Variety, Visualization
Veracity, Volume, Velocity only
6-1.e: Big Data 3 Vs: (1) Volume — data too large for traditional databases (terabytes/petabytes); (2) Velocity — generated at high speed (real-time sensor data, social media); (3) Variety — structured (tables), semi-structured (Json, Xml), unstructured (text, images, video). Some frameworks add Veracity (accuracy) and Value as additional Vs, but the original 3 Vs are the core exam concept.

6-1.q: Diagnostic analytics differs from Descriptive analytics in that diagnostic analytics:
o: Only reports what happened
Investigates Why something happened — identifying root causes, correlations, and contributing factors
Predicts future outcomes
Recommends optimal decisions
6-1.e: Descriptive: reports on what happened (historical facts). Diagnostic: investigates Why — uses drill-down, data mining, correlations, and root cause analysis to explain the 'why' behind the numbers. Example: sales dashboard shows revenue dropped 15% (descriptive) → diagnostic analysis reveals the drop was concentrated in one region where a competitor launched (diagnostic root cause).

6-1.q: An ERP system integrates business functions by using:
o: Separate databases for each department that sync nightly
Spreadsheets shared via email
A Single Shared Database that all modules (finance, HR, supply chain, sales) access in real-time
Paper-based processes supplemented by IT systems
6-1.e: ERP's core advantage: One Shared Database accessible by All modules in Real-Time. When a sales order is entered, inventory is immediately reduced, Ar is updated, and revenue is recognized — all automatically. This eliminates data silos and re-entry errors. Traditional systems used separate department databases that required manual reconciliation — creating delays and errors.

6-1.q: Structured data differs from Unstructured data in that structured data is:
o: Always more valuable than unstructured data
Organized In A Defined Format (rows and columns) that can be directly queried with SQL
Generated faster than unstructured data
Only used for financial reporting
6-1.e: Structured data: organized in predefined schemas (tables, rows, columns) — directly queryable with SQL. Examples: accounting transactions, customer records, inventory counts. Unstructured data: no predefined format — text, emails, images, videos, social media posts. Unstructured data represents ~80% of all data generated but requires advanced tools (Nlp, image recognition) to analyze. Semi-structured (Json, Xml) falls between.

6-1.q: A Data Warehouse is Best described as:
o: A real-time transaction processing system
A collection of raw, unprocessed data in any format
A Cleaned, Structured Repository of historical data from multiple source systems — optimized for Analytical Queries and reporting
A departmental database for one business unit
6-1.e: Data Warehouse: (1) Subject-Oriented — organized around business subjects (sales, finance, customers); (2) Integrated — consolidated from multiple source systems; (3) Non-Volatile — historical data retained, not overwritten; (4) Time-Variant — tracks changes over time. Optimized for complex analytical queries (Olap), not real-time transactions (Oltp). Source: Bill Inmon's definition.

6-1.q: In the context of data analytics, a Dashboard primarily provides:
o: Raw data exports for manual analysis
Detailed transaction-level reports
A Visual Display Of Key Metrics And KPIs enabling at-a-glance monitoring of performance
Statistical models for predicting outcomes
6-1.e: Dashboards are visual management tools: graphical displays of Key Performance Indicators and metrics that enable managers to quickly assess performance without reviewing detailed reports. Good dashboards: use appropriate chart types, highlight exceptions, enable drill-down for detail, update in near-real-time. They are primarily Descriptive analytics tools.

6-1.q: Predictive analytics uses which of the following techniques to forecast future outcomes?
o: Only historical trend charts
Excel pivot tables exclusively
Statistical Models, Machine Learning, and Regression Analysis using historical patterns to forecast future events
Simple arithmetic averages
6-1.e: Predictive analytics employs: regression analysis, time series forecasting, machine learning models, neural networks, and probabilistic models. It uses historical patterns to Predict future outcomes. Finance examples: demand forecasting, cash flow prediction, credit default probability, fraud likelihood scoring. The accuracy of predictions depends on data quality and model appropriateness.

6-1.q: The Velocity dimension of Big Data refers to:
o: The accuracy and reliability of the data
The total amount of data stored
The Speed At Which Data IS Generated And Must Be Processed — real-time or near-real-time data streams
The different formats of data (text, images, video)
6-1.e: Velocity: the high Speed at which data is generated and needs to be processed. Examples: stock market tick data (milliseconds), IoT sensor data (continuous), social media posts (millions/minute), credit card transactions (real-time fraud detection). High velocity requires stream processing technologies (Apache Kafka, Spark Streaming) rather than batch processing.

6-1.q: Cybersecurity in a technology context is Most important for finance because:
o: Finance teams use the most advanced technology
Finance data is publicly available anyway
Financial Data (account numbers, payment details, forecasts) is among the most sensitive — breaches cause direct financial loss, regulatory penalties, and reputational damage
Cybersecurity only affects IT departments
6-1.e: Finance systems contain the most sensitive and directly monetizable data: bank accounts, payment credentials, financial forecasts, M&A information. Breaches cause: Direct Loss (fraudulent transfers, ransomware payments), Regulatory Penalties (SOX violations, Gdpr fines), Reputational Damage, and Competitive Harm (leaked M&A plans). Finance professionals must understand cyber risks that affect their data and systems.

6-1.q: Which analytics type asks the question 'Why did sales decline last quarter?'
o: Descriptive analytics
Diagnostic Analytics — investigating root causes of past events
Predictive analytics
Prescriptive analytics
6-1.e: Diagnostic analytics: answers 'Why did this happen?' It uses techniques like drill-down analysis, data mining, correlation analysis, and root cause investigation to explain historical performance. The sales decline question requires investigating: which products/regions declined, what competitive or market factors changed, whether pricing or promotion changes contributed. This is diagnostic, not descriptive (just reporting the decline).

6-1.q: An Emerging Technology that allows finance professionals to analyze contracts, extract key terms, and automate compliance checks using natural language processing is:
o: Blockchain
Rpa
Cloud computing
Artificial Intelligence / Nlp — reading and understanding unstructured text documents
6-1.e: Nlp (Natural Language Processing) — a branch of AI — enables systems to Read, Understand, and Extract Information from unstructured text. Finance applications: contract analysis (automatically extracting payment terms, obligations, risk clauses from thousands of contracts), regulatory filing review, earnings call analysis, customer sentiment from surveys. This eliminates weeks of manual review.

6-1.q: The concept of Data-Driven Decision Making means that management decisions are based on:
o: Management intuition and experience exclusively
The most recent industry analyst reports
Analysis Of Relevant Data And Evidence rather than intuition alone — using analytics to support and validate decisions
Only financial statement data
6-1.e: Data-Driven Decision Making: using Data Analysis and empirical evidence to guide decisions, reducing reliance on gut feeling or experience alone. In finance: using regression analysis to set pricing, using customer analytics to allocate marketing spend, using variance analysis to prioritize operational improvements. This doesn't eliminate judgment — it informs and improves it.

6-1.q: Which type of analytics would a CFO use to recommend the Optimal capital structure for the next fiscal year?
o: Descriptive analytics — reporting current capital ratios
Diagnostic analytics — explaining why leverage changed
Predictive analytics — forecasting future interest rates
Prescriptive Analytics — optimization models recommending the best capital mix given constraints and objectives
6-1.e: Prescriptive analytics: recommends the Best Action given objectives and constraints. A capital structure optimization model: inputs = cost of debt, cost of equity, tax rates, credit ratings, covenant constraints; objective = minimize WACC or maximize firm value; output = recommended debt/equity ratio. This is the most sophisticated analytics tier — combining prediction with optimization to recommend action.

6-2.q: Master Data Management (Mdm) primarily aims to:
o: Eliminate the need for databases
Create a Single Authoritative Source ('golden record') for key business entities like customers, vendors, and products
Back up all company data to the cloud
Automate all transaction processing
6-2.e: Mdm creates a Single 'golden record' for core business entities — preventing conflicting data across systems. This improves data consistency, eliminates duplicates, and ensures all business units work from the same trusted data.

6-2.q: Which data quality dimension is violated when the same customer appears twice in the database with slightly different names?
o: Accuracy
Timeliness
Uniqueness — duplicate records violate this dimension
Validity
6-2.e: Uniqueness: Each real-world entity should be represented Once in the database. Duplicate customer records violate uniqueness. Mdm and deduplication processes address uniqueness violations.

6-2.q: ETL stands for:
o: Encryption, Transfer, Logging
Extract, Transform, Load — the process of moving data from source systems to a data warehouse
Enterprise Technology Licensing
Error Testing and Logging
6-2.e: ETL = Extract data from source systems → Transform it (clean, standardize, aggregate) → Load into a data warehouse for analysis. The Transform step is where data quality issues are addressed.

6-2.q: Poor data quality in financial reporting most directly leads to:
o: Improved audit efficiency
Stronger internal controls
Unreliable Management Decisions and potential financial statement errors
Faster month-end close
6-2.e: Poor data quality = 'garbage in, garbage out.' Decisions based on inaccurate, incomplete, or inconsistent data are unreliable. In financial reporting, data quality failures can lead to material misstatements and flawed budgets.

6-2.q: The Data Steward role is best described as:
o: The executive accountable for data quality in a business domain
The IT professional managing physical data storage and security
The Operational role responsible for day-to-day data quality management, definitions, and standards
The Cdo who sets enterprise data strategy
6-2.e: Data Steward: operational, day-to-day manager of data quality — defines data standards, monitors quality metrics, resolves data issues for a specific data domain. Distinguished from: Data Owner (executive), Data Custodian (IT storage), and Cdo (enterprise strategy).

6-2.q: Data Accuracy as a quality dimension means:
o: Data is available when needed
Data has no duplicate records
Data Correctly Represents The Real-World Entity Or Event it is meant to capture — free from errors
Data is stored in a consistent format
6-2.e: Accuracy: data correctly reflects reality. Example: a customer's address field contains their actual current address (not an old address). Inaccurate data leads to: wrong invoices, failed deliveries, incorrect financial reports, poor decisions. Controls: validation rules at data entry, data cleansing programs, periodic verification against authoritative sources.

6-2.q: Data Completeness as a quality dimension means:
o: All data is accurate and error-free
Data is always available to users
All Required Data Fields Are Populated — no mandatory attributes are missing
Data is stored in only one location
6-2.e: Completeness: all required data elements are present. Example: a sales transaction with a missing customer Id or an order with no quantity. Incomplete data breaks downstream processes: an invoice with no shipping address cannot be fulfilled; a transaction with no Gl account cannot be posted. Controls: mandatory field validation, completeness checks at data entry.

6-2.q: ETL stands for which data integration process?
o: Evaluate, Test, Load
Extract, Transform, Load — the process of moving data from source systems to a data warehouse
Enable, Transfer, Link
Encrypt, Transmit, Log
6-2.e: ETL: (1) Extract — pull data from source systems (ERP, CRM, spreadsheets); (2) Transform — clean, standardize, and restructure data (date formats, code conversions, deduplication, calculations); (3) Load — push the transformed data into the target system (data warehouse, data mart). ETL is the backbone of data warehouse population and must be controlled to ensure data integrity.

6-2.q: Master Data Management (Mdm) is designed to solve which data problem?
o: Slow transaction processing speeds
Multiple Conflicting Versions of critical reference data — customers, products, suppliers — across different systems (no single source of truth)
Too much data being stored
Insufficient data backup procedures
6-2.e: Mdm problem: Company A has 'Ibm Corp' in their CRM, 'Ibm' in ERP, and 'International Business Machines' in accounts payable — all the same customer, but the systems don't know that. Mdm creates a Golden Record — a single, authoritative, reconciled record — and synchronizes it across all systems. Without Mdm, analytics produces unreliable results because the same entity is counted multiple times or differently.

6-2.q: A Data Steward in a data governance program is responsible for:
o: Setting the overall data governance policy for the entire organization
Performing database administration and technical maintenance
Ensuring Data Quality And Compliance for a Specific Data Domain — the business representative accountable for day-to-day data quality
Auditing all IT systems for compliance
6-2.e: Data Steward: the business-side owner of a Specific Data Domain (e.g., customer data, product data, financial data). Responsibilities: defining data quality rules for their domain, monitoring data quality metrics, resolving data quality issues, approving data changes, ensuring compliance with data policies. Distinct from the Data Owner (executive accountability) and technical Dba (system maintenance).

6-2.q: Data Timeliness as a quality dimension means:
o: Data is accurate and error-free
Data has no duplicate records
Data IS Available When Needed for decision-making — not too old to be useful
Data is stored in a consistent format across systems
6-2.e: Timeliness: data is available and current When Needed. If monthly financial data takes 10 days to close, managers make decisions without the most current picture. Modern finance strives for continuous accounting and real-time data. Controls: monitoring data latency (time from transaction to availability), automated feeds from source systems, real-time dashboards.

6-2.q: Data Consistency means that:
o: Data is always 100% accurate
All data fields are filled in
Data IS Uniform Across Systems And Time — the same data element represents the same value in every system where it appears
Data is never duplicated
6-2.e: Consistency: the same data element has the Same Value across all systems that use it. Example: customer 'John Smith' should show the same address in the CRM, ERP, and billing system. Inconsistency occurs when systems are updated independently without synchronization. Mdm and ETL processes are key controls for maintaining consistency.

6-2.q: The Data Lifecycle includes which of the following phases?
o: Only creation and deletion
Creation → Storage → Use → Archival → Deletion — managing data from birth to disposal
Only creation and use
Storage and reporting only
6-2.e: Data Lifecycle: (1) Creation/Capture — data entry, automated feeds; (2) Storage — databases, cloud; (3) Use — analytics, reporting, transactions; (4) Archival — moved to cheaper storage for compliance retention; (5) Deletion/Destruction — secure disposal when retention period expires. Governance must cover All phases — especially secure Deletion (Gdpr right to erasure requires proper disposal).

6-2.q: A Data Catalog in data governance serves as:
o: A backup copy of the database
A list of all data governance policies
An Inventory of available data assets — describing what data exists, where it lives, its meaning, owner, and quality — enabling users to find and understand data
A security access control system
6-2.e: Data Catalog: a metadata management tool that documents: what data assets exist, where they are located, their business definitions (data dictionary), data owners, quality metrics, and usage information. Enables: data discovery (analysts can find the right data), impact analysis (what breaks if this changes), compliance documentation. Without a catalog, organizations waste time searching for data or use the wrong data.

6-2.q: Data Validity as a quality dimension means:
o: Data is available when needed
Data has no missing fields
Data Values Conform To The Defined Format, Type, And Business Rules — only acceptable values are stored
Data has been verified by an external source
6-2.e: Validity: data conforms to defined rules. Examples: a date field contains only valid dates (not '99/99/9999'); a country code is from the approved list; an account balance is within permitted range. Controls: input validation rules at point of entry, domain constraints in database design, integrity checks during ETL. Invalid data breaks downstream processes and calculations.

6-2.q: The Primary difference between a Data Owner and a Data Steward is:
o: There is no practical difference — both do the same work
Data owners work in IT; data stewards work in business
Data Owner = Executive Accountability for a data domain (sets policy, allocates resources); Data Steward = Operational Responsibility for day-to-day data quality within that domain
Data stewards have more authority than data owners
6-2.e: Data Owner: senior executive (e.g., CFO for financial data) — sets Data Policy, allocates resources for data quality, is ultimately accountable for the domain. Data Steward: operational staff member — implements the owner's policies day-to-day, monitors quality, resolves issues, approves changes. Both are Business roles (not IT). Together they form the business accountability structure for data quality.

6-2.q: Data Uniqueness (non-duplication) as a quality dimension prevents which specific problem?
o: Incorrect data entry by users
Data being unavailable when needed
Duplicate Records — the same entity (customer, vendor, product) appearing multiple times, causing inflated counts and incorrect analytics
Data stored in the wrong format
6-2.e: Uniqueness: each entity appears exactly Once in the system. Duplicate customer records cause: double-counting in analytics, sending duplicate invoices (customer relations issue), incorrect Ar balance (collecting twice or missing a balance). Controls: deduplication algorithms in Mdm, unique key constraints in database design, matching rules during data loads.

6-2.q: A Data Quality Scorecard in a governance program measures:
o: The number of IT security incidents per month
Compliance With Data Quality Rules across each quality dimension (accuracy, completeness, etc.) — providing a metric-based view of data health
The speed of transaction processing
Employee data entry productivity
6-2.e: Data Quality Scorecard: tracks Quantitative Metrics for each quality dimension: % of records with complete required fields, % of addresses validated against postal database, % of records with consistent values across systems. Provides objective evidence of data quality trends, identifies problem areas, and demonstrates ROI of data governance investments. Similar to a financial dashboard but measuring data health.

6-2.q: The business purpose of Data Governance is Best described as:
o: A purely technical IT function for database management
Restricting access to sensitive data
Establishing Accountability, Policies, And Processes to ensure data is trustworthy, consistent, and used appropriately throughout the organization
Archiving old data to reduce storage costs
6-2.e: Data Governance: the overall management of data availability, usability, integrity, and security. Business purpose: ensure data is a Reliable Asset that can be trusted for decision-making, regulatory compliance, and operational processes. Without governance: analytics produce conflicting results, regulators find data quality violations, and business decisions are made on inaccurate information.

6-2.q: When preparing a Data Warehouse, the Transform step in ETL primarily involves:
o: Copying data exactly as it appears in the source system
Loading data into the target database
Cleaning, Standardizing, And Restructuring data — converting formats, applying business rules, deduplicating, and calculating derived fields
Backing up the source system data
6-2.e: Transform is the most complex ETL step: (1) Clean — remove nulls, fix errors; (2) Standardize — convert date formats, normalize codes (e.g., 'US', 'USA', 'United States' → 'US'); (3) Restructure — reorganize from operational schema to analytical schema; (4) Calculate derived fields; (5) Deduplicate; (6) Apply business rules. Poor transformation = 'garbage in, garbage out' — analytics built on poorly transformed data will be unreliable.

6-3.q: The primary advantage of an ERP system over separate departmental systems is:
o: Lower initial cost of implementation
Each department maintains its own independent data
All departments share a Single integrated database — providing real-time, consistent data across the enterprise
Easier customization for each department
6-3.e: ERP's core advantage: One integrated database shared by all modules. When a sale is recorded in Sd, it instantly updates Fi, inventory in Mm, and commissions in HR. This eliminates data silos and provides real-time visibility.

6-3.q: A company implementing ERP that runs the old and new systems simultaneously is using which approach?
o: Big bang implementation
Phased rollout
Parallel Running — safest approach but most expensive
Agile implementation
6-3.e: Parallel Running: Both old (legacy) and new (ERP) systems operate simultaneously. If ERP fails, the business continues on legacy. This is the Safest approach but Most Expensive because both systems must be maintained simultaneously.

6-3.q: The Biggest risk of a 'Big Bang' ERP implementation is:
o: It takes too long to implement
It is the most expensive option
All business processes go live simultaneously — a major failure could disrupt the Entire organization at once
It requires extensive parallel running
6-3.e: Big Bang: all modules go live on One date. If the system has serious problems, the Entire company is affected simultaneously with no fallback. Despite this risk, companies sometimes choose big bang to avoid extended transition costs.

6-3.q: Segregation of duties in an ERP system is primarily enforced through:
o: Physical separation of computer terminals
Role-Based Access Controls — users assigned roles limiting which transactions they can perform
Separate databases for each department
Manual supervisor reviews
6-3.e: In ERP, Sod is enforced through Role-Based Access Control (Rbac). Each user is assigned roles that define which transactions they can initiate, approve, and view — preventing one person from controlling all phases of a transaction.

6-3.q: Which ERP module handles accounts payable, accounts receivable, and the general ledger?
o: Materials Management (Mm)
Human Resources (HR)
Sales & Distribution (Sd)
Financial Accounting (Fi)
6-3.e: Financial Accounting (Fi) module: manages all core financial accounting — general ledger, accounts payable, accounts receivable, fixed asset accounting, and financial statement generation.

6-3.q: The Core distinguishing feature of an ERP system compared to standalone applications is:
o: ERP systems are always cloud-based
ERP systems are cheaper than standalone applications
A Single Integrated Database shared by all modules — eliminating data silos and enabling real-time visibility across the organization
ERP systems require no customization
6-3.e: ERP's fundamental advantage: One Shared Database. When a sales order is entered, inventory automatically decrements, Ar updates, revenue recognizes, and production schedules adjust — all in real-time with No Manual Rekeying between systems. Standalone systems have separate databases requiring periodic synchronization — creating delays, reconciliation effort, and data inconsistencies.

6-3.q: A 'Big Bang' ERP implementation means:
o: Implementation occurs over several years in phases
Only the finance module is implemented first
All Modules Go Live Simultaneously across the entire organization on a single cutover date
The ERP replaces only legacy financial systems
6-3.e: Big Bang: All modules implemented at Once, entire organization switches on one date. Highest Risk (if it fails, everything fails), but fastest time to benefit and avoids maintaining dual systems. Used by organizations with strong project management, standardized processes, and high risk tolerance. Alternative: phased approach (modules one at a time) or pilot approach (one location first).

6-3.q: The Parallel Running approach to ERP implementation is the Safest because:
o: It is the fastest approach
It requires the least investment
Both Old And New Systems Run Simultaneously — results are compared until the new system is verified, then the old is decommissioned
It uses no customization
6-3.e: Parallel Running: old legacy system and new ERP both process the same transactions Simultaneously. Results are compared — if they match, confidence in the new system increases. Safest because if the new system fails, the old system continues. Most Expensive: maintaining two complete systems, double data entry, significant reconciliation effort. Used for high-stakes implementations where failure risk is unacceptable.

6-3.q: ERP Customization refers to modifying the ERP's Source Code to fit specific business processes. The Primary risk of customization is:
o: Users may prefer the standard functionality
Customizations make the system more expensive initially
Customizations Must Be Re-Done at each upgrade — significantly increasing the long-term cost of ownership and complexity of upgrades
Vendors may charge extra for customization support
6-3.e: ERP Customization risk: when the vendor releases an upgrade, All customizations must be Re-Implemented in the new version — every upgrade becomes a major project. Best practice: configure using built-in parameters (change the Configuration, not the Code), modify business processes to fit the ERP ('vanilla implementation'), and reserve customization only for absolutely critical requirements.

6-3.q: An ERP system Improves internal controls over standalone systems primarily by:
o: Reducing the number of employees needed
Eliminating the need for an internal audit function
Automating Controls (approval workflows, segregation of duties enforced by system roles, automatic 3-way matching, audit trails) that previously required manual enforcement
Making financial reports more visually appealing
6-3.e: ERP Internal Control benefits: (1) Role-Based Access — system enforces Sod automatically; (2) Automated Approval Workflows — payments over threshold automatically route for approval; (3) System-Enforced 3-Way Match — blocks payment unless Po, receipt, and invoice match; (4) Audit Trails — every change recorded with user, timestamp; (5) Automated Reconciliations. These controls are more consistent than manual controls.

6-3.q: The Phased Approach to ERP implementation involves:
o: All modules going live at once
Running old and new systems simultaneously
Implementing Modules Or Locations One At A Time — reducing risk by learning from each phase before the next
Outsourcing implementation to the vendor
6-3.e: Phased Approach: implement the ERP in Stages — either module by module (finance first, then supply chain, then HR) or location by location (one country, then the next). Lower Risk than big bang (lessons learned in each phase applied to next), but Slower to full benefit realization and requires managing integration between live ERP modules and still-live legacy systems during the transition.

6-3.q: ERP Master Data includes which of the following?
o: Daily transaction records like individual invoices
Month-end financial statements
Foundational Reference Data that drives transactions — customer master, vendor master, chart of accounts, product master, employee master
System error logs
6-3.e: ERP Master Data: the foundational reference data that all transactions reference. Examples: customer master (name, address, credit terms, payment terms), vendor master (address, bank account, payment terms), chart of accounts, product master (price, description, Gl mapping). Master Data Quality is critical — an error in a vendor's bank account number could misdirect millions in payments.

6-3.q: Which ERP module directly supports the Procure-To-Pay process?
o: General Ledger module
Human Resources module
Procurement / Accounts Payable module — managing purchase orders, receipts, vendor invoices, and payments
Fixed Assets module
6-3.e: Procurement/AP Module handles the procure-to-pay cycle: requisitions → purchase orders → goods receipt → vendor invoice matching → payment processing → vendor master management. Integration: automatically creates Gl journal entries, updates inventory (Materials Management module), and feeds the financial statements. Covers the Expenditure transaction cycle from an Ais perspective.

6-3.q: In ERP terminology, a 'Chart Of Accounts' is:
o: A listing of all customer accounts
The Master List of all general ledger account codes used to record and classify financial transactions
A report showing account balances
The coding structure for vendor invoice processing
6-3.e: Chart Of Accounts (Coa): the master list of all Gl accounts — each with a unique code and name. Defines how transactions are classified and reported. Designing the Coa properly is one of the most critical ERP implementation decisions — it drives all financial reporting, consolidation, and analysis. Changes to the Coa after go-live are complex and disruptive.

6-3.q: An ERP system's Audit Trail capability means that:
o: The system automatically audits itself for errors
External auditors have direct access to the system
Every Transaction Change IS Recorded with the user Id, timestamp, and before/after values — providing a complete history
The system prevents unauthorized transactions
6-3.e: ERP Audit Trails: every Create, Modify, or Delete operation is logged with: (1) Who made the change (user Id); (2) When (timestamp); (3) What changed (before and after values); (4) From Where (terminal/Ip address). This is critical for: fraud detection, SOX compliance, audit investigations, and resolving disputed transactions. Audit trails should be Protected — even database administrators should not be able to delete them.

6-3.q: ERP System Risks include all of the following Except:
o: Significant implementation cost and time (often exceeds budget)
Change management challenges as employees resist new processes
Integration challenges with legacy systems
Elimination Of All Internal Control Risks — ERP systems make all controls automatic and failure-proof
6-3.e: ERP does Not eliminate all control risks. ERP Risks: (1) Cost overruns — implementations often exceed budget significantly; (2) Change Management — employee resistance, training gaps; (3) Customization costs; (4) Data Migration errors; (5) Vendor dependency; (6) Over-Reliance on system controls (if system controls are wrong or bypassed, all transactions are affected); (7) Single point of failure. ERP requires careful governance.

6-3.q: The Change Management aspect of ERP implementation refers to:
o: Technical changes to the system code
Regulatory changes that affect ERP configuration
Managing The Human Side of implementation — communicating, training, addressing resistance, and helping employees adapt to new processes and systems
Changing the chart of accounts during implementation
6-3.e: Change Management in ERP: ERP implementations fail most often not due to technical issues but Human factors. Change management: (1) Communicate early and clearly why change is needed; (2) Involve users in design; (3) Train thoroughly before go-live; (4) Address resistance and concerns; (5) Celebrate early wins. Organizations that treat ERP as a technical project rather than a business transformation project consistently have worse outcomes.

6-3.q: What does the term 'Vanilla Implementation' mean in ERP?
o: Implementing only the financial modules
Implementing the ERP with maximum customization
Using The ERP'S Standard Functionality With Minimal Customization — adapting business processes to fit the software rather than modifying the software
Implementing only for a single business unit
6-3.e: Vanilla Implementation: using the ERP as designed by the vendor, with minimal customization. Benefits: (1) Lower Cost — no custom development; (2) Easier Upgrades — no customizations to maintain; (3) Faster implementation; (4) Access to vendor improvements in future releases. Requires: businesses to re-examine and adapt their processes to align with ERP best practices. This is often the recommended approach for non-differentiating processes.

6-3.q: ERP Integration with external systems (e.g., bank feeds, Edi with suppliers) is accomplished through:
o: Manual data re-entry from external systems
Quarterly data uploads via Usb drives
APIs, Edi Connections, and Middleware — automated interfaces that exchange data between the ERP and external systems in real-time or scheduled batches
Paper-based processes that are later entered
6-3.e: ERP Integration: (1) APIs (Application Programming Interfaces) — real-time data exchange with cloud applications; (2) Edi (Electronic Data Interchange) — standardized electronic document exchange with suppliers/customers (purchase orders, invoices); (3) Middleware — integration platforms that translate between different data formats. Automated integration eliminates manual re-keying, reduces errors, and enables real-time data flow.

6-3.q: When an ERP implementation Fails or significantly underperforms, the Most Common root cause is:
o: The ERP software had too many bugs
The hardware infrastructure was inadequate
Poor Change Management and Inadequate User Training — employees cannot or will not use the new system effectively
The vendor provided insufficient support
6-3.e: ERP Failure statistics: most ERP failures and cost/time overruns trace to People And Process issues, not technical problems. Root causes: (1) inadequate Change Management (employees bypass new system); (2) insufficient User Training; (3) Poor Data Quality in migration; (4) Scope Creep; (5) Unrealistic Timelines. The technology often works — the failure is in managing the human and organizational change required.

6-4.q: The Primary difference between a Data Warehouse and a Data Lake is:
o: Data warehouses are cloud-based; data lakes are on-premise
Data Warehouses store structured, cleaned data for BI reporting; Data Lakes store raw structured and unstructured data for advanced analytics
Data warehouses are larger than data lakes
Data lakes are used only for financial data
6-4.e: Data Warehouse: structured, cleaned, integrated data — for BI reporting by business analysts. Data Lake: raw data in native format (structured + unstructured) — for data scientists doing advanced analytics.

6-4.q: Olap (Online Analytical Processing) is optimized for:
o: Recording individual transactions quickly
Analyzing Large Datasets — aggregating and querying historical data for decision support
Real-time payment processing
Individual record updates
6-4.e: Olap is optimized for Analytical Queries over large volumes of historical data. Oltp (ERP) is optimized for Recording individual transactions. Running Olap queries on an Oltp system degrades transaction performance — that's why organizations build separate data warehouses.

6-4.q: The Drill-Down capability in a BI dashboard allows a user to:
o: Compare budget to actual variances
Filter data by date range only
Navigate From Summary Data To Underlying Detail
Export data to Excel
6-4.e: Drill-Down = navigating from aggregated summary data to progressively more detailed levels. Example: Total revenue → by region → by product line → by individual customer. This identifies the specific source of a variance or trend.

6-4.q: A Data Mart differs from a full Data Warehouse in that a data mart:
o: Stores raw, unprocessed data
Is used only for transaction processing
Is a Subject-Specific Subset of the data warehouse for a single department
Has lower data quality
6-4.e: Data Mart: a Subset of the data warehouse focused on a specific business area — e.g., a Finance data mart, a Marketing data mart. Faster to query, easier for departmental users, typically fed from the enterprise data warehouse.

6-4.q: The Balanced Scorecard links KPIs to strategy across which four perspectives?
o: Financial, Customer, Internal Process, Learning & Growth
Revenue, Cost, Quality, Speed
Sales, Operations, HR, Finance
Past, Present, Future, External
6-4.e: Balanced Scorecard (Kaplan & Norton) four perspectives: (1) Financial — ROI, revenue growth; (2) Customer — satisfaction, retention; (3) Internal Process — cycle time, quality; (4) Learning & Growth — training, innovation. The BSC links these KPIs to the company's strategic objectives.

6-4.q: A Data Lake differs from a Data Warehouse in that a data lake:
o: Contains only financial data
Is smaller and faster than a data warehouse
Stores Raw, Unprocessed Data in any format (structured, semi-structured, unstructured) — intended primarily for data scientists and advanced analytics
Is optimized for transactional processing
6-4.e: Data Lake: stores All data types Raw and unprocessed — structured (tables), semi-structured (Json, log files), unstructured (documents, images, video). Schema On Read — structure is applied when data is read, not when stored. Used by data scientists for advanced analytics, machine learning. Data Warehouse: structured, cleaned, schema on write, used by business analysts for reporting. Risk of data lake: can become a 'data swamp' without proper governance.

6-4.q: A Data Mart is Best described as:
o: A large data store containing all enterprise data
The raw data storage layer for machine learning
A Subset Of The Data Warehouse focused on a Specific Business Unit or Subject Area (e.g., finance data mart, sales data mart)
A real-time transaction processing system
6-4.e: Data Mart: a subset of the data warehouse, scoped for a specific Department or subject area. Finance data mart: contains only financial data relevant to finance analysts. Benefits: faster query performance (smaller data set), tailored to departmental needs, easier to govern. Can be built top-down (from existing warehouse) or bottom-up (independently, then integrated). Multiple data marts can coexist.

6-4.q: Olap (Online Analytical Processing) is primarily used for:
o: Recording real-time transactions (sales, payments, orders)
Multi-Dimensional Analysis of historical data — slicing, dicing, drilling down, and rolling up data across multiple dimensions
Performing bank reconciliations
Managing day-to-day business operations
6-4.e: Olap enables: Slice (filter one dimension — show only Q3), Dice (filter multiple dimensions — Q3, North America, Product A), Drill-Down (from region to state to city), Roll-Up (from city to region to country), Pivot (rotate the analysis view). Used for trend analysis, budget variance, profitability by product/customer/geography. Queries are complex but run against pre-aggregated cubes for speed.

6-4.q: Oltp (Online Transaction Processing) differs from Olap in that Oltp is optimized for:
o: Complex multi-dimensional analysis
Historical reporting across years of data
Fast, High-Volume Recording Of Individual Transactions — optimized for speed and consistency of transactional operations
Aggregated summary reporting
6-4.e: Oltp: designed for Transactional workloads — thousands of short, concurrent Insert/Update/Delete operations (sales orders, payments, inventory movements). Optimized for: speed, concurrency, data consistency (Acid properties). ERP systems are Oltp. Olap systems are separate — running complex analytical queries on Oltp's historical data would slow down live operations. Hence: ERP (Oltp) → ETL → Data Warehouse → Olap queries.

6-4.q: A Key Performance Indicator (KPI) dashboard is an example of which type of analytics?
o: Predictive analytics — forecasting future KPIs
Prescriptive analytics — recommending actions
Descriptive Analytics — showing current and historical performance against defined metrics
Diagnostic analytics — explaining why KPIs changed
6-4.e: KPI Dashboards are Descriptive analytics: they show What IS Happening (or has happened) through visual displays of metrics. A dashboard showing revenue vs. budget, days sales outstanding, inventory turns — describes current state. If the dashboard also shows Why revenue missed budget (root cause analysis), that element is diagnostic. Pure dashboards = descriptive.

6-4.q: Drill-Down capability in a BI tool allows users to:
o: Create complex statistical models
Send reports to other users automatically
Navigate From Summary To Detail — clicking on a regional total to see the underlying individual transactions or subsidiary breakdowns
Filter data to a specific time period only
6-4.e: Drill-Down: hierarchical navigation from Summary → Detail. Click on 'North America revenue: $50M' → see by country → click Canada ($8M) → see by province → click Ontario ($3M) → see individual transactions. This enables analysts to investigate anomalies without pre-building separate detailed reports. Essential for variance analysis and root cause identification in financial reporting.

6-4.q: A Self-Service BI tool allows business users to:
o: Access the production transaction database directly
Create Their Own Reports And Analyses without IT support — using visual, drag-and-drop interfaces
Program custom algorithms and machine learning models
Replace the need for a data warehouse
6-4.e: Self-Service BI: empowers Business Users (finance analysts, managers) to create their own reports, dashboards, and analyses using intuitive visual tools (Power BI, Tableau, Qlik) without requiring IT or developer support. Benefits: faster insights (no IT queue), better adoption (users design what they need), reduced IT burden. Risks: inconsistent definitions, data governance challenges, 'shadow analytics' on ungoverned data.

6-4.q: In BI reporting, a Balanced Scorecard organizes KPIs around which four perspectives?
o: Financial, Operational, IT, and HR
Financial, Customer, Internal Processes, and Learning & Growth — Kaplan and Norton's framework for strategy execution
Revenue, Costs, Assets, and Liabilities
Short-term, Medium-term, Long-term, and Strategic
6-4.e: Balanced Scorecard (Kaplan & Norton): organizes strategy execution around four perspectives: (1) Financial — how do we look to shareholders? (ROI, EVA, revenue growth); (2) Customer — how do customers see us? (satisfaction, retention, Nps); (3) Internal Processes — what must we excel at? (cycle time, quality); (4) Learning & Growth — can we continue to improve? (employee skills, system capabilities). Balances financial and non-financial metrics.

6-4.q: Predictive Analytics in BI tools uses which type of historical data relationship to forecast future values?
o: Random number generation
Simple extrapolation of the most recent data point only
Statistical Patterns And Correlations in historical data — regression, time series, and ML models identify relationships to forecast future outcomes
Expert opinion without data
6-4.e: Predictive Analytics in BI: uses statistical models to identify Patterns in historical data and project them forward. Techniques: linear/multiple regression (identify causal factors), time series decomposition (trend + seasonality + cyclical), machine learning models (identify non-linear patterns). Example: predicting next quarter's revenue using historical revenue, marketing spend, economic indicators, and seasonality patterns.

6-4.q: The purpose of Data Visualization in business analytics is to:
o: Store data in a visual format for archival
Replace the need for data analysis
Communicate Insights Effectively by representing data graphically — enabling pattern recognition and decision support that tables of numbers cannot provide
Create decorative reports for executive presentations
6-4.e: Data Visualization: representing data graphically to reveal patterns, trends, and insights that are Hidden In Tables Of Numbers. Good visualization: chooses the Right chart type (scatter for correlation, bar for comparison, line for trend, heat map for density), eliminates chart junk, enables quick interpretation. Bad visualization misleads (truncated axes, inappropriate chart types). The goal: communication, not decoration.

6-4.q: An Operational Report differs from a Management Report in that operational reports are:
o: More strategic and higher-level
Generated only for the board of directors
Detailed, Transaction-Level Reports used in Day-To-Day Operations — aging schedules, daily cash positions, inventory movement reports
Reviewed only on a quarterly basis
6-4.e: Operational Reports: detailed, transaction-level, used daily by operational staff (Ar aging for collections team, daily cash position for treasury, open purchase orders for procurement). Management Reports: higher-level aggregates, trends, and KPIs for strategic decision-making (monthly P&L for CFO, quarterly business review for CEO). Both use BI tools but serve different audiences and decision levels.

6-4.q: Predictive Modeling in finance requires which prerequisite data condition?
o: Data must be less than one year old
Data must be stored only in a data warehouse
Sufficient Volume Of Historical Data With Consistent Quality — the model learns patterns from historical data, which must be representative of future conditions
Data must be audited by an external accountant
6-4.e: Predictive models learn from Historical Patterns — they require: (1) Sufficient Volume (thousands to millions of records for statistical significance); (2) Historical Depth (enough history to capture cycles, seasonality); (3) Consistent Quality (missing or inaccurate data produces biased models); (4) Relevance (historical patterns must be applicable to future conditions). Garbage data → unreliable predictions.

6-4.q: A Heat Map in data visualization is Best used to show:
o: Revenue trends over time
Correlation between two variables
Density Or Intensity across two dimensions — such as transaction volume by hour of day and day of week
Hierarchical breakdown of costs
6-4.e: Heat Maps: color-coded grids showing Density or Intensity across two dimensions. Finance uses: fraud detection heat maps (high-risk transaction patterns by time and geography), customer purchase patterns (products × customer segments), seasonal demand heat maps. The color intensity immediately reveals high and low concentration areas that would be invisible in a table of numbers.

6-4.q: Real-Time BI dashboards differ from traditional scheduled reports because they:
o: Are more expensive and therefore more reliable
Only display financial data
Update Continuously As New Transactions Occur — giving managers current-state visibility rather than a snapshot from a batch run
Require no data governance or quality controls
6-4.e: Real-Time BI: dashboards connected to live data sources that Update as transactions occur (or with minimal latency, e.g., seconds or minutes). Benefits: immediate detection of anomalies (fraudulent transactions, inventory stockouts, cash position changes), faster response to operational issues. Traditional batch reporting (overnight ETL) means managers see yesterday's data. Real-time requires more robust infrastructure.

6-4.q: The difference between a Report and an Analytic Insight is that an insight:
o: Is always created automatically by BI software
Contains more rows of data than a report
Interprets The Data — explaining What IT Means and Why IT Matters for decision-making, not just presenting the numbers
Is generated faster than a report
6-4.e: Reports present data; Insights interpret it. A report says 'Q3 revenue was $48M vs. $50M budget.' An insight says 'Q3 revenue missed by $2M — concentrated in Emea where currency headwinds reduced dollar-reported revenue by $3M; constant currency performance was actually $1M above budget. No action needed on sales, but treasury should review Fx hedging.' Insights guide Decisions; reports provide the evidence.

6-5.q: In Y = a + bX for cost estimation, 'a' represents:
o: The variable cost per unit
The coefficient of determination
The Fixed Cost Component — the Y-intercept (cost when X = 0)
The slope of the regression line
6-5.e: Y = a + bX: 'a' = Fixed Cost (Y-intercept — the cost when activity level X = 0). 'b' = Variable Cost per unit of activity (slope). 'X' = the activity level (cost driver).

6-5.q: R² = 0.80 in a regression means:
o: The model has 80 data points
80% of cost variation is explained by the selected cost driver
The fixed cost is 80% of total cost
The model is 80% accurate
6-5.e: R² (coefficient of determination): R² = 0.80 means 80% of the variation in total cost is explained by the selected cost driver. Higher R² = better fit = more reliable cost estimation.

6-5.q: Stratified Sampling involves:
o: Selecting every nth item from a list
Selecting entire geographic clusters
Dividing the population into subgroups and taking a Random Sample from each group
Giving every item an equal chance of selection
6-5.e: Stratified Sampling: divide population into meaningful Subgroups (strata) and randomly sample from Each stratum. This ensures all subgroups are represented. Used in auditing when different risk levels apply to different subsets.

6-5.q: A Type I Error occurs when:
o: Failing to reject a false null hypothesis
The sample size is too small
Rejecting A True Null Hypothesis — concluding a difference exists when it actually does not (false positive)
The p-value exceeds 0.05
6-5.e: Type I Error = False Positive: you Reject the null hypothesis when it is actually True. Type Ii Error = False Negative: you Fail To Reject the null hypothesis when it is actually False.

6-5.q: Which chart type is Best for showing monthly revenue trends over 24 months?
o: Pie chart
Bar chart
Scatter plot
Line Chart — best for trends over continuous time periods
6-5.e: Line Chart is optimal for showing Trends Over Time — continuous data points connected by a line make upward/downward trends visually obvious. Bar charts compare discrete categories. Pie charts show parts of a whole. Scatter plots show correlation between two variables.

6-5.q: In the regression equation Y = a + bX used for cost estimation, 'a' represents:
o: Variable cost per unit
The slope of the cost line
The Fixed Cost Component — the Y-intercept, representing total costs when X (activity) = 0
The coefficient of determination
6-5.e: Y = a + bX: Y = total cost; a = Fixed Cost (Y-intercept — the cost when activity X = 0); b = Variable Cost Per Unit (slope — how much total cost increases per unit of activity); X = activity level (units, hours, etc.). Example: Y = $50,000 + $15X means fixed cost = $50,000, variable cost = $15 per unit. This is the foundational cost estimation model on the CMA exam.

6-5.q: The Coefficient Of Determination (R²) in regression analysis measures:
o: The slope of the regression line
The total number of data points used
The Percentage Of Variation In The Dependent Variable (Y) explained by the independent variable(s) (X) — ranges from 0 to 1
The fixed cost estimate
6-5.e: R²: measures Goodness Of Fit. R² = 0.85 means 85% of the variation in total costs is explained by the variation in the activity driver (e.g., machine hours). Higher R² = better fit. R² = 1 = perfect fit. R² = 0 = no relationship. For cost estimation: R² > 0.90 is desirable. Low R² suggests the chosen cost driver doesn't explain costs well — try a different driver.

6-5.q: Stratified Sampling ensures that:
o: Every item in the population has an equal chance of selection
The largest items by dollar value are always selected
The Sample Contains Representatives From Each Subgroup (stratum) in proportion to their presence in the population
Items are selected in sequential order
6-5.e: Stratified Sampling: population is divided into Subgroups (strata) — e.g., invoices by dollar amount range — and a proportional sample is drawn from each stratum. Benefits: ensures representation of all subgroups, reduces sampling error for heterogeneous populations. Finance use: audit sampling where large invoices need higher representation than small ones. Alternative: simple random sampling (equal probability for all items).

6-5.q: A Type I Error in hypothesis testing (false positive) means:
o: Failing to reject a false null hypothesis
Correctly rejecting the null hypothesis
Rejecting A Null Hypothesis That IS Actually True — concluding there IS an effect when there isn't one
Accepting a true null hypothesis
6-5.e: Type I Error (False Positive): Reject the null hypothesis when it IS actually true. Example: concluding a cost driver IS significant when it isn't (spurious correlation). Type Ii Error (False Negative): Fail To Reject the null when it is actually False — missing a real effect. In auditing: Type I = rejecting a fair balance as misstated; Type Ii = accepting a materially misstated balance. The acceptable Type I rate = significance level (α).

6-5.q: Cluster Sampling in data analytics involves:
o: Selecting every nth item from an ordered list
Drawing samples from each predefined subgroup
Dividing The Population Into Groups (clusters) and randomly selecting Entire Clusters — then examining all items within selected clusters
Selecting only the largest items
6-5.e: Cluster Sampling: population divided into Clusters (natural groupings like branches, departments, geographic regions). A random Sample Of Clusters is selected, then All items within selected clusters are examined. More efficient when population is geographically dispersed (no need to travel everywhere — only to selected clusters). Less statistically precise than stratified sampling. Used for branch audit sampling.

6-5.q: Time Series Decomposition in analytics separates time series data into which components?
o: Mean, median, and mode
Revenue, cost, and profit
Trend, Seasonal, Cyclical, and Irregular components — enabling more accurate forecasting by modeling each component separately
Fixed and variable elements
6-5.e: Time Series Decomposition: (1) Trend — long-term direction (up, down, flat); (2) Seasonal — regular patterns within a year (retail Q4 spike, summer slow season); (3) Cyclical — multi-year economic cycles (recession, expansion); (4) Irregular — random noise. By separating these components, forecasters can model each independently and produce more accurate combined forecasts than treating the series as one undifferentiated pattern.

6-5.q: Multiple Regression differs from Simple Regression in that multiple regression:
o: Uses less data
Is always more accurate
Uses Two Or More Independent Variables to explain variation in the dependent variable — capturing more complex cost relationships
Is only applicable to non-linear cost relationships
6-5.e: Multiple Regression: Y = a + b₁X₁ + b₂X₂ + ... Allows Multiple Cost Drivers simultaneously. Example: total overhead = $20,000 + $8(machine hours) + $12(# of setups). Each driver independently contributes to explaining costs. Better fit (higher R²) than simple regression when multiple drivers exist. Risk: multicollinearity (if X₁ and X₂ are correlated with each other, results are unreliable).

6-5.q: A Scatter Diagram (scatter plot) in cost analysis is used to:
o: Show the distribution of data across categories
Track performance over time with a line chart
Visualize The Relationship between a cost driver (X) and total costs (Y) — each point is one period's data, helping assess whether a linear relationship exists before running regression
Display the composition of costs in a pie chart
6-5.e: Scatter Diagram: plots Individual Data Points (X = activity level, Y = total cost). Before running regression, inspect the scatter plot: (1) Is there a Linear relationship or is it curved? (2) Are there Outliers that might distort regression? (3) Is the relationship Strong (tight clustering around a line) or Weak (widely dispersed)? Regression on non-linear data or data with outliers produces misleading results.

6-5.q: The High-Low Method of cost estimation uses which two data points?
o: The first and last periods in the data
The average of the highest and lowest months
The Period With The Highest Activity and The Period With The Lowest Activity — computing variable cost as the change in cost ÷ change in activity
The median and mean of the dataset
6-5.e: High-Low Method: Variable cost = (Cost at highest activity − Cost at lowest activity) ÷ (Highest activity − Lowest activity). Then: Fixed cost = Total cost − (Variable cost × Activity). Simple to calculate but Unreliable: uses only 2 data points (ignores all others), and the high/low periods may be Outliers. Regression is statistically superior. The high-low method is a quick estimate, not a precise analysis.

6-5.q: In data visualization, a Line Chart is Most appropriate for showing:
o: Composition of a whole (parts of 100%)
Comparison of values across categories at one point in time
Trends Over Time — how a metric changes from period to period
Correlation between two variables
6-5.e: Appropriate Chart Selection: Line Chart = best for Trends Over Time (continuous data across periods). Pie/Donut = composition (% of total). Bar/Column = comparison across categories. Scatter Plot = correlation between two variables. Waterfall = incremental changes. Using the wrong chart type obscures rather than reveals insights. A pie chart for time trends, or a line chart for categorical comparison, confuses rather than communicates.

6-5.q: Benford'S Law applied to a vendor invoice dataset would be Suspicious if:
o: The digit '1' appears as the first digit about 30% of the time
Most invoice amounts are round numbers ending in '00'
Invoices Starting With '9' Appear Far More Frequently than Benford's Law predicts — suggesting amounts just below a $10,000 approval threshold
Invoice amounts are mostly below $1,000
6-5.e: Benford's Law: digit '1' = ~30%, '2' = ~18%, '9' = ~5% as first digit in natural data. Fraudsters creating fictitious invoices below an approval threshold (e.g., $9,500, $9,800, $9,950) produce abnormally high frequency of '9' as first digit. This pattern is detectable with Benford analysis and should trigger investigation. Normal business data approximates Benford's distribution.

6-5.q: Hypothesis Testing in analytics determines:
o: The exact value of an unknown population parameter
The most likely forecast for next period
Whether Observed Data Provides Sufficient Evidence To Reject A Null Hypothesis about a population at a specified confidence level
The best visualization technique for the data
6-5.e: Hypothesis Testing: starts with a Null Hypothesis (H₀ — assumed true unless evidence shows otherwise) and Alternative Hypothesis (H₁). Calculates a test statistic and p-value. If p-value < significance level (α, typically 0.05), Reject H₀ and accept H₁. Finance applications: testing whether a new pricing strategy significantly increased margins, whether two cost centers have significantly different cost structures.

6-5.q: Random Sampling ensures that:
o: Large items are always included in the sample
Items are selected based on their risk level
Every Item In The Population Has An Equal Probability of being selected — eliminating selection bias
The most recently processed items are sampled
6-5.e: Random (Simple Random) Sampling: each item in the population has an Equal and Independent chance of selection. Produces an Unbiased sample that is statistically representative of the population. Basis for most statistical inference. Limitation: all items (large and small) equally likely — may under-represent important high-value items. In audit sampling, often combined with stratification.

6-5.q: Moving Average analysis in time series is used to:
o: Predict future turning points in the business cycle
Calculate the correlation between two variables
Smooth Out Short-Term Fluctuations to reveal the underlying Trend — averaging data over a rolling window
Identify fixed and variable cost components
6-5.e: Moving Average: calculates the average of the last N periods (e.g., 3-month or 12-month moving average). Each new period adds the latest value and drops the oldest. Result: Smoothed trend line that reduces the visual noise of seasonal swings and random fluctuations, making the underlying trend direction clearer. 12-month moving average eliminates seasonal effects entirely.

6-5.q: The Correlation Coefficient (r) measures which relationship between two variables?
o: Whether one variable Causes changes in the other
The Percentage of variation explained by the regression
The Strength And Direction of the Linear Relationship between two variables — ranges from -1 (perfect negative) to +1 (perfect positive)
The statistical significance of the regression
6-5.e: Correlation Coefficient (r): measures how strongly two variables move Together. r = +1 = perfect positive correlation (X increases → Y increases proportionally). r = -1 = perfect negative correlation. r = 0 = no linear relationship. Note: correlation ≠ causation. R² (coefficient of determination) = r² = % of variation explained. In cost analysis: high |r| between driver and cost suggests the driver explains costs well.

6-6.q: A Phishing attack primarily works by:
o: Overloading a server with excessive traffic
Intercepting network communications
Deceiving users through fake emails to steal credentials or trigger malicious actions
Injecting malicious code into databases
6-6.e: Phishing: deceptive Emails or websites that appear legitimate trick users into clicking malicious links, entering credentials on fake login pages, or authorizing fraudulent wire transfers. It is the #1 entry point for cyberattacks.

6-6.q: Multi-Factor Authentication (Mfa) requires:
o: A very long password
Two Or More verification factors: something you know, something you have, something you are
Changing your password every 30 days
Using different passwords for each system
6-6.e: Mfa requires Two Or More independent factors from different categories. Combining factors means a stolen password alone cannot grant access. Mfa is one of the most effective cybersecurity controls.
